name: Nuclear Physics
description: Nuclear structure, fission, fusion, radioactive decay, and nuclear reactions. Nuclear physics explores the atomic nucleus — protons and neutrons bound by the strong nuclear force. It explains the stability of matter, the energy of stars, the decay of radioactive elements, and provides the basis for nuclear energy and medical imaging.
axioms: Strong nuclear force: the residual interaction between nucleons (protons and neutrons); short-range (~1 fm), spin-dependent, charge-independent
Nuclear binding energy: B(Z,N) = [Zm_p + Nm_n - m_nucleus]c² — the mass defect is the energy that holds the nucleus together
Radioactive decay: N(t) = N₀e^(-λt); half-life t₁/₂ = ln 2/λ — exponential decay is universal
Fission: a heavy nucleus (e.g., U-235) absorbs a neutron and splits, releasing ~200 MeV per fission — chain reaction possible
Fusion: light nuclei combine to form a heavier nucleus + energy — the energy source of stars and thermonuclear weapons
Nuclear shell model: nucleons occupy orbitals; magic numbers (2, 8, 20, 28, 50, 82, 126) indicate closed shells and enhanced stability
Liquid drop model: the nucleus behaves like an incompressible charged liquid drop — explains fission and binding energy trends (Bethe-Weizsäcker formula)
reasoning_patterns: Binding energy curve analysis: the binding energy per nucleon peaks at Fe-56 (~8.8 MeV/nucleon) — fission releases energy for A > 56, fusion for A < 56
Decay chain tracking: follow radioactive series (U-238, Th-232, U-235) through successive alpha and beta decays to stable lead isotopes
Cross-section computation: predict reaction rates from nuclear matrix elements — critical for reactor design and nucleosynthesis
Shell model calculation: fill nuclear orbitals following the nuclear Pauli principle, predict ground-state spin, parity, and magnetic moment
Neutron transport: model neutron diffusion, slowing down, and multiplication in reactor cores — Boltzmann transport equation
Beta decay analysis: classify β⁻ (n → p + e⁻ + ν̄_e), β⁺ (p → n + e⁺ + ν_e), and electron capture
Alpha decay: tunneling through the Coulomb barrier — Geiger-Nuttall law relates decay energy to half-life
boundary_conditions: Does not cover nuclear engineering (reactor design, safety systems, fuel cycle optimization)
Does not cover health physics or radiation protection in full detail
Quark-gluon plasma requires particle physics and QCD at high temperature/density
Nuclear astrophysics (r-process, s-process, p-process nucleosynthesis) overlaps with astrophysics domain
The equation of state of nuclear matter at high density is not fully constrained (e.g., neutron star interiors)
Exotic nuclei far from the valley of stability have short half-lives and are poorly characterized
key_references: Ring & Schuck. The Nuclear Many-Body Problem
IAEA Nuclear Data Services — NuDat, ENDF/B library
Rolfs & Rodney. Cauldrons in the Cosmos
canonical_truths: E = mc² applies directly to nuclear binding energy — the mass defect is real, measurable energy
Iron-56 has the highest binding energy per nucleon (~8.8 MeV) — the most stable nucleus
Nuclear fission of U-235 releases ~200 MeV per fission event — compared to ~4 eV per chemical reaction
Solar energy comes from the pp-chain fusion: 4p → ⁴He + 2e⁺ + 2ν_e + 26.73 MeV
Half-lives span ~54 orders of magnitude: from 10⁻²² s to 10³² years (Te-128)
The neutron is stable inside a nucleus (bound by the strong force) but decays in free space in ~15 minutes
Magic-number nuclei are anomalously stable — the doubly magic ⁴⁰Ca, ⁴⁸Ca, ²⁰⁸Pb are milestones in nuclear structure
